findings.title: Runner.getMethods() rebuilt per-stock (× 2) inside evaluateAllStocks() loop
findings.description: Inside the per-snapshot for-loop in evaluateAllStocks() the code invokes Runner.getMethods() twice per stock: once inside the trends IIFE (L88) and once for passCount (L98). Each call to getMethods() in methods/runner.js (L66-81) executes METHODS.filter(...).filter(...).map(...) which performs ~3× METHODS.length array iterations plus a fresh allocation of N≈83 method-meta objects, AND inside .map() invokes MT.getType() + MT.isDefaultActive() for every method (REGISTRY hash lookups). This was flagged by the previous performance audit as F-PF-001 (severity 'high', 2026-05-15) and re-flagged here because the fix was NEVER applied — the code at L88/L98 still reads `for (const m of Runner.getMethods())`. A near-identical leftover in detect-changes.js L303 calls it once for the orphan-method cleanup (acceptable — not in a hot loop).
findings.mechanism: Per stock: 2 × (filter + filter + map over 83 methods + 83 × 2 REGISTRY lookups) ≈ 500 ops. Across 15,734 watchlist tickers: ~7.9M wasted operations + ~31k transient object arrays for GC. Memory churn is meaningful because each call allocates ~83 fresh small objects.
findings.reproducer: node -e "const R=require('./methods/runner.js'); console.time('x'); for(let i=0;i<3500;i++) R.getMethods(); console.timeEnd('x');" — single-digit-ms today × 2 calls × ~4.5× universe-expansion (3.5k → 15.7k) is non-trivial in CPU + GC time.
findings.impact: Doubles the methods-report stock-loop CPU time. At 15k tickers projects to several seconds of pure wasted work plus measurable GC pressure that competes with the parallel methods-history snapshot writes.
findings.suggested_fix: Hoist a single `const ALL_METHODS = Runner.getMethods();` above the for-loop (already done at L1190 for the rendering pass), then pass that constant into both the trends IIFE and the passCount loop. Trivial diff, was the literal fix recommended by the prior audit.

findings.title: Snapshots written with pretty-print JSON (null, 2) on every full + price-only pull
findings.description: Three writeFileAtomic call sites in pull-yahoo serialize snapshots with `JSON.stringify(obj, null, 2)`: L1383 _priceOnlyUpdate (~80% of daily pulls), L1711 full-pull canonical write, and L1768 delisted-flag rewrite. Each pretty-printed snapshot is ~30-40% larger than the compact form and stringify is ~1.5-2× slower because of all the indent + newline insertion. Counter-evidence elsewhere in the same file shows the team has consciously moved away from pretty-print where it mattered (fundamentals-cache write L1531 uses no indent, manifest writes L1829/L1831 use no indent, snapshot-methods-history.js F-PF-005 explicitly documents the optimization). The big snapshot files were missed.
findings.mechanism: At 15,734 tickers × ~50KB per snapshot: ~1.5× larger on-disk footprint (~250MB extra), ~30-40% slower stringify per call. Multiplied by the daily-pull rewrite cadence and the methods-history loader downstream reads these files back at full size, the overhead compounds across the pipeline. The price-only path is the hottest one — it's the 80% fast-path that the whole frequency-trennung architecture (Tag 166) was designed to keep cheap.
findings.reproducer: node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const s=JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('snapshots/NVDA.json','utf8')); console.time('p'); JSON.stringify(s,null,2); console.timeEnd('p'); console.time('c'); JSON.stringify(s); console.timeEnd('c');" — typical 1.5-2× ratio.
findings.impact: At 19k+ tickers the full-pull I/O becomes a measurable fraction of the 163-min Yahoo Pull wall-clock; price-only fast-path slowdown is a direct hit on the 80% of stocks per run. Storage is also a real issue — snapshots/ at 15k × 50KB pretty = ~750MB, compact = ~520MB (~30% saving). Git diff size shrinks proportionally.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop `, null, 2` from L1383, L1711, L1768. Snapshots are machine-consumed (no human reads 50KB JSON). If git-diff readability is desired, run `jq .` on demand. This is the same pattern that fixed pull-historical-prices OOM at Tag 222.
findings.compound_hint: Same anti-pattern as the historical-prices OOM fix at Tag 222 — pretty-print of large JSON in hot loops.

findings.title: loadStocks() in generate-modes-report / generate-screener does serial sync readFileSync over entire snapshots dir
findings.description: Both report generators load the full snapshots directory via `files.map(f => JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(...)))` — pure serial blocking I/O. snapshot-methods-history.js L29-45 and detect-changes.js L352-365 already moved to async batched fs.promises.readFile loaders (Tag 223c F-PF-007/009) — a proven pattern in this codebase. The two HTML report generators were left behind and still block the event loop reading 50KB × 15k files serially.
findings.mechanism: Per-file: openSync → read full ~50KB into Buffer → close → JSON.parse. At a typical 8-10ms per file on SSD (cold cache), 15,734 files ≈ 130-160s of pure blocking I/O wall-time per generator. Across modes + screener + methods-report that's 3× the same penalty in a single pipeline run.
findings.reproducer: Compare the F-PF-009 sync-readFileSync-in-Promise.all anti-pattern note at snapshot-methods-history.js L25-28 — the same code shape was already shown to be a no-op for parallelism. modes/screener loaders share the exact problem without the Promise.all wrapper.
findings.impact: At 15k tickers, each generator adds ~2-3 minutes of avoidable serial I/O wall-time to the pipeline. Three generators × 3 minutes = ~9 minutes of waste in the daily critical path. Multiplied by the 4.5× universe expansion this is a meaningful slice of the 14.6h projection.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace loadStocks() with the same async-batch pattern already proven in snapshot-methods-history.js loadFilesAsync() — BATCH=200, Promise.all over fs.promises.readFile. Copy-paste fix (the helper is short).
findings.compound_hint: Same fix shape as F-PF-007/009 in snapshot-methods-history.js.

findings.title: Runner.evaluateStock() does 4-5 redundant full scans over the results map per stock
findings.description: After the main `for (const m of METHODS)` loop fills `results`, evaluateStock() calls (a) MT.isDisqualifiedByDataGuards(results) which iterates Object.entries(results) and calls MT.isDataGuard for each entry (→ getType → REGISTRY lookup), (b) Object.keys(results).filter(MT.isCore) to count CORE methods, (c) the same Object.keys(results).filter(MT.isCore && pass) for coreCountPass. Then evaluateStockLegacy returns out.results to most callers — which DISCARDS coreCount/coreCountPass entirely. So 2-3 full-map scans + 2× full Object.keys allocations are computed and thrown away for almost every caller (generate-modes-report.js evaluateAll, generate-methods-report.js evaluateAllStocks, snapshot-methods-history.js, detect-changes.js, generate-screener.js).
findings.mechanism: Per stock: ~83 results entries × 3 scans × MT.getType REGISTRY lookups ≈ 250 wasted hash lookups + 2 throwaway intermediate arrays. Across 15,734 tickers × ~5 generators-that-call-evaluateStock per pipeline run ≈ 20M wasted ops + ~75k intermediate Object.keys allocations for GC.
findings.reproducer: Inspect runner.js L57-63 vs evaluateStockLegacy L83-86 — the legacy wrapper exported as Runner.evaluateStock strips coreCount/coreCountPass/disqualified before returning. Every published caller uses the legacy export.
findings.impact: MEDIUM because each scan is cheap individually (small REGISTRY map), but the work is computed on every code path and thrown away. Lazy compute via getters or splitting into a separate evaluateStockExtended that callers opt into would eliminate it. Materializes as a few hundred ms across the pipeline at 15k tickers.
findings.suggested_fix: Two options: (a) make coreCount/coreCountPass lazy getters on the returned object so the work only runs if the caller reads them; (b) split into evaluateStockSimple (results only — what the legacy wrapper exposes) and keep the extended version for the one caller that actually needs the metadata. Today evaluateStockExtended is only consumed by tag28-tests.js per grep — option (b) is safe.

findings.title: writeManifestIncremental scans the growing results array on every 100-ticker checkpoint (O(N²/100))
findings.description: writeManifestIncremental fires every 100 tickers from the worker pool and runs `results.filter(r => r && (r.status === 'ok' || r.status === 'price-only')).length` over the entire `results` array. results grows linearly with completed tickers. At 15,734 tickers and 157 checkpoint flushes, total work is sum(100, 200, ..., 15700) ≈ 1.24M filter iterations + 157 atomic writeFileAtomic operations. Each filter rebuilds a transient intermediate array.
findings.mechanism: O(N²/CHECKPOINT_INTERVAL). At N=3.5k, 100-step: ~60k filter ops — invisible. At N=15.7k: ~1.24M ops + ~157 transient arrays of growing size, each allocated and then discarded. CI memory pressure also rises proportionally because each writeFileAtomic stringifies the slim manifest object (small, that part is fine).
findings.reproducer: node -e "const a=[]; console.time('x'); for(let i=0;i<15700;i++){a.push({status:'ok'}); if(i%100===0) a.filter(r=>r.status==='ok').length;} console.timeEnd('x');" — tens of ms today, becomes seconds at 100k.
findings.impact: MEDIUM. Real but not dominant. Bigger concern is that each checkpoint also writes a ~150-byte file 157 times via atomic tmp+rename — fine in isolation but adds fsync pressure on CI runners.
findings.suggested_fix: Maintain two running counters (n_ok, n_skipped_mcap) updated in-place inside processOne() when a result is pushed. writeManifestIncremental reads the counters in O(1). Eliminates the filter scan entirely. Same pattern used elsewhere in the codebase for counter-based stats.

findings.title: method-history-state.json (14MB) loaded twice — once in generate-methods-report, once in detect-changes
findings.description: method-history-state.json is the committed sidecar holding per-ticker × per-method trend history (currently 14MB per the task brief). generate-methods-report.js L50-58 reads + JSON.parses it once at the top of evaluateAllStocks(). detect-changes.js _loadMethodHistory() reads + parses it again in the same pipeline run. Each load is ~150-300ms wall-time of synchronous I/O + parse. The two scripts run sequentially in CI; the second parse is purely duplicate work because the file is unchanged between them. Worse: at 19k tickers the brief notes the file will scale ~5× to ~70MB and JSON.parse cost scales linearly.
findings.mechanism: JSON.parse of a 14MB document takes ~150-300ms on a modern node + materializes a large in-memory hash. Doing it twice doubles the cost. At 70MB (projected) it's ~750ms-1.5s each parse × 2 = up to 3 seconds of duplicate work + transient ~150MB heap footprint per parse.
findings.reproducer: Time-stamp the entry/exit of _loadMethodHistory in both scripts during a real pipeline run and observe the duplicate work.
findings.impact: MEDIUM today, HIGH at 19k. The bigger issue is heap pressure: parsing 70MB JSON spikes node heap to ~200-300MB transient — on a CI runner already loading 15k×50KB snapshots, this contributes to OOM risk the audit Tag 226b is warning about.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) pass the parsed object between steps via a process-shared cache file (snapshot to a process-temp .pickle), or more simply (b) refactor so generate-methods-report and detect-changes share a thin loader module that memoizes the parse — same lib/ pattern used for atomic-write.js. Even simpler: pipeline both steps into a single node process so module-scope memo across the require()d modules is automatic.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with F-PF-002 (snapshot pretty-print) and the general pipeline-heap pressure — JSON.parse of the 14MB sidecar contributes to total node heap residency.

findings.title: generate-screener.js per-stock readScoreHistory does synchronous file read inside buildRow loop
findings.description: buildRow() is called inside `for (const s of stocks)` at L3009 in main(). Inside buildRow() at L320 it calls readScoreHistory(ticker) which does fs.readFileSync(score-history/<ticker>.json). The function memoizes via _scoreHistoryCache so the SECOND call for the same ticker is free, but in a one-pass build each ticker is read once → 15,734 separate sync open/read/close/parse cycles serialized on the main thread. The cache mitigates only re-reads which don't happen in this generator's single-pass build.
findings.mechanism: Same sync-readFileSync-in-hot-loop anti-pattern as F-PF-003 but on a different directory. score-history files are typically <5KB so each read is fast (~1-2ms), but at 15,734 × 1-2ms = 15-30s of pure blocking I/O wall-time on top of the parallel snapshot load.
findings.reproducer: Add timing around L3009 buildRow loop and observe the cumulative readScoreHistory overhead.
findings.impact: MEDIUM. Smaller files than snapshots but still serial blocking I/O over the full universe. At 15k tickers it adds ~15-30s to the screener build wall-time.
findings.suggested_fix: Pre-load all score-history files in a single async batched loader (same pattern as F-PF-003 fix) at main() start, then have readScoreHistory consult the pre-built Map. Memo cache becomes the index. Removes the sync I/O from the hot loop entirely.
findings.compound_hint: Same root cause as F-PF-003 — serial sync FS reads in the per-ticker hot loop.

findings.title: eligibleForMode/topAllMust recomputed for each mode in main()'s diagnostic loop after buildHtml already did the same work
findings.description: buildHtml() at L524-525 iterates SM.MODES keys and computes `eligibleForMode(evaluated, m)` per mode, then calls topAllMust(...) internally. main() at L1219-1223 then re-runs the entire eligibleForMode + topAllMust dance per mode purely to print a console line (`${modeId}: ${eligible.length} eligible, ${allMust.length} all-MUST-pass`). Each eligibleForMode walks all `evaluated` (~15k stocks) running SM.isExcludedBySector + dataGuards check; topAllMust filters by modeEvals.passed and sorts.
findings.mechanism: Wasted O(N × M_modes) ≈ 15,734 × 5 = ~78,670 extra filter operations + 5 extra sort calls just for console logging. With ~85 method results per stock the inner SM.isExcludedBySector hits ~5-10 fields each → not trivial.
findings.reproducer: Note that the result of L525 eligibleByMode[m] is computed inside buildHtml and discarded when buildHtml returns. main() then recomputes from scratch.
findings.impact: MEDIUM. Adds avoidable wall-time at 15k+. The buildHtml function could return a tiny stats object as a side-channel for main() to log without recomputing.
findings.suggested_fix: Change buildHtml() to return { html, stats: { perMode: { HYPERGROWTH: { eligibleCount, allMustCount }, ... } } } and have main() print from stats. Zero recomputation.

findings.title: Methods-report HTML stringifies & deep-walks all 15k rows × 83 methods for sector-distribution + per-method Top-N
findings.description: renderHTML at L176-194 walks all rows once to build sectorMap (OK, single pass). But the per-method Top-N section at L225-236 then iterates `for (const m of methods)` and inside each iteration does `discoveryRows.filter(r => r.results[m.id].computable && Number.isFinite(r.results[m.id].value))` AND a sort — i.e. 83 methods × full-rows filter + sort = O(M × N log N). At N=15,734 stocks × M=83 methods × log2(15734)≈14 ≈ 18M comparisons per render, plus 83 intermediate arrays each potentially ~15k entries (~1.2M total transient elements). The audit-reports/2026-05-15.md at L347-351 already flagged a sibling instance (F-PF-002 'rows.filter() twice per method') that has been partially addressed but the per-method Top-N path still pays the full O(M × N log N) cost.
findings.mechanism: Each filter rebuilds a new array of computable rows for method m, then sorts it. Most methods have similar computable sets, so 83 × ~12k = ~1M transient array entries → significant GC pressure when run in series. Sort comparators are simple numeric so per-comparison cost is low, but the count is the issue.
findings.reproducer: Time the L225-236 loop on a 3.5k-row run vs simulate a 15k-row run with mock data. Linear-in-rows scaling; with sort it's O(N log N) per method.
findings.impact: MEDIUM at 3.5k, HIGH at 15k. Could push methods-report render time from seconds to tens-of-seconds. Combined with the 9.73MB embedded HTML output this is a real bottleneck in the report-generation phase.
findings.suggested_fix: One pass over rows that, per method, maintains a bounded min/max heap of size TOP_N (50). Drops the per-method filter+sort to O(N log TOP_N) instead of O(N log N), and avoids 83 transient arrays. Or simpler: after one full pass building per-method arrays, sort each (cheap since each array is at most N entries) — at least the full-row filter is amortized.
findings.compound_hint: Same hot-loop class as F-PF-001 (per-stock waste in methods-report).

findings.title: loadFiles in generate-modes-report uses filter().map() — minor double iteration
findings.description: loadStocks does `.filter(...).map(...).filter(x => x !== null && typeof x === 'object' && !Array.isArray(x))` — two filter passes and a map over the full file list. Cosmetic compared to the sync-readFileSync inside the map (which is the real F-PF-003 issue), but the trailing post-map filter walks the full 15k-element array a third time.
findings.mechanism: 3× O(N) walks over the file array + transient intermediate arrays. With async batch load (F-PF-003 fix), this can be folded into the batch reducer naturally.
findings.reproducer: Read L82-87 directly — the pipe is visible.
findings.impact: LOW. Folded into the F-PF-003 fix at zero extra cost.
findings.suggested_fix: When refactoring to async batches per F-PF-003, validate + push in a single pass.
